course_title: Managing Cultural Heritage: Ethics, History and Practice
course_description:
ARCS3118 is a work Integrated learning course that prepares students for work in the cultural heritage sector. Heritage is increasingly recognised as a key element of human culture and is an important element of identity, power and authority. Managing heritage in the face of a range of pressures is a daily reality for professional archaeologists. It requires a nuanced understanding of what constitutes heritage, how heritage values are ascribed, legal structures controlling management practices, stakeholder values and professional ethical obligations – items that often conflict with each other in heritage practice! ARCS3118 delves into the theory, process and practice of heritage management through a combination of seminars, online modules, class exercises and fieldtrips to explore current best practice.

Class approach: The approach in class is participatory, along with class discussions to help students understand CHM issues at hand. The idea is that students come prepared to ask questions and get involved in group discussions. The classes are very focused on workplace skills, and it is strongly recommended that students attend and use the time to ask questions and understand the topics.

Readings: The readings for the class range from academic texts to legislation and international agreements. You must become familiar with key professional documents such as The Burra Charter, including its guidance notes, to successfully complete the assessments.

Field trips: The field trips will take place during class time and involve visiting nearby heritage places, recording significant elements and discussing management issues. There are no costs associated with the fieldtrips. You just have to get yourself to the designated starting place for each trip by the starting time.

Technical requirements: You will undertake technical training in GIS at your own pace over a five week period. This will be assessed. We are using QGIS as an open-access GIS platform.

Assessment: The course has assessment items, two relate to the online modules and one to the in-class content. Learning activities (class time, online modules) plus learning materials (online sources and readings) are all closely inter-related.

prerequisites: We recommend completing the following courses before enrolling in this one:
2 units Level 1 course and 4 units Level 2 courses
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

course_aims: ARCS3118 aims to introduce students to the principles and practices of contemporary cultural heritage management, providing an international and historically grounded perspective on the topic. The course also aims to develop key employability skills including understanding legislation, how legislation applies to heritage problems, use of geographical information systems (GIS) and how to write a significance assessment and cultural heritage management plans meeting legislative and professional expectations in Queensland.

learning_outcomes.description: Be familiar with the ethics, history and legislative framework of cultural heritage management across Australia and Queensland
Recognise and apply the principles of The Burra Charter to heritage places
Apply Geographical Information Systems (GIS) methods to cultural heritage management
Explain significance-based cultural heritage management practices within a critical framework, emphasising the importance of community
Communicate heritage concepts and findings for a variety of industry appropriate purposes, while demonstrating cultural safety

assessment_details.task_description: You will be required to complete a sequence of class exercises reflecting the week's theme and learning outcomes. The work will be completed in-class and submitted at the end of the class. Class activities will take a variety of written formats, incorporating a combination of groupwork, individual tasks and self reflection. Each class exercise is worth 10%. There are eight classes across the semester where in-class exercises will be assessed. Your best five scores will count towards your course grade. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course. AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. assessment_details.title: Heritage management briefing document and meeting
assessment_details.due_date: Due 3pm 20/05/2026 21/05/2026 - 29/05/2026 Part I (the briefing document) is due at 3pm on 20/05/2026. Select dates and times will be made available to students to complete Part II. Students will need to assign themselves a timeslot to complete their presentation to teaching staff between 21/05/2026 and 29/05/2026.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is designed to replicate standard industry tasks in assessing heritage places and communicating best practice strategies for management. The target audience for your briefing document and presentation is a heritage professional working on behalf of the local community of that place. You will provide the guidance that the heritage professional will take back to the community for implementation. You should assume the local community wish to preserve the values of the place as best they can with limited funding. Your management strategies can either offer alternative uses for the site, or work with the ways the place is already being used. Many of the in-class exercises will provide you with the skills to complete this task; use the feedback provided on those to guide your approach here. PART I: Management briefing document Select a place that is NOT on any heritage register, list or overlay at the local, state, national or international levels. Visit the place and make first-hand observations about it, including photographing significant elements. The place will need to be accessible to the public or in a place you can freely access. Do not speak with any community members or anyone else associated with the place for your assessment. Your work will need to be supported by some research into the history of the place, but only enough to support your significance assessment and management strategies (it is not the focus of the task). Produce a short management briefing document that: 1. Describes the place (including its boundaries, community and key visitors, history and site elements); 2. Assesses its significance using the Queensland Heritage Act 1992 criteria; 3. Proposes three realistic values-based, heritage management policies for the place. The word count is no more than 1500 words. The format should be a briefing document that clearly and professionally conveys the proposed management strategies for the place. You must include a GIS-created map to support your work; you may wish to convey the place boundary, heritage elements, significance and/or management strategies as a map. Your brief needs to include a reference list (including image sources). Your briefing document will be submitted to Turnitin in advance of a presentation. PART II: Presenting your management plan To present your findings you will give a 4-minute summary of your brief, followed by 4 minutes of informal questions and discussion about the work. The format will emulate a client meeting and gives you a chance to explain your work, particularly justifying and advocating for your heritage management policies. You should broadly follow the structure of the briefing document in your summary, focusing on the key parts and your reasoning. You can assume your audience has read your brief. A copy of the brief will be made available to you in the meeting, which you can refer to and guide the discussion. You will not be able to present a Powerpoint or other media in the meeting. Recording of Assessment: Assessment Procedure When a student is required to complete an oral or practical assessment item worth 20% or more, the performance will be recorded and retained as per Appendix 1 as the official recording of the item for re-mark purposes. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed for the purposes of: i. moderation of marking ii. provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or iii. re-marking following a successful re-mark application. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course. AI Use: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. You must submit an AI usage statement (template provided in tutorial and on Blackboard). Even if you did not use AI to support your writing, you must still submit the AI usage statement. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your assignment electronically by the due time, on the due date.
Your assignment must be submitted via Turnitin on blackboard. To submit your assignment electronically log in to https://learn.uq.edu.au/ultra with your UQ username and password, then click on Course Code>>Assessment>>Assignments, and use the appropriate assignment submission link for each piece of assessment. No e-mailed submissions of assessments will be accepted.
By uploading your assignment via Turnitin, you are certifying that the work you submit is your own work except where correctly attributed to another source. Do not submit your assignment if it contains any work that is not your own.
You are required to retain proof of submission of your assessment. Your Digital Receipt is available for download from your Assignment Dashboard. If you cannot see your submission and download your digital receipt, your assessment has not been successfully submitted; please submit again.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties with Blackboard, please contact the ITS Support Team .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note that from Semester 2, 2025 the Assessment Procedure has changed. You must submit your application on or before the assessment due date and time.
Grounds of an Extension
A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. All extension requests must be accompanied by documentation that supports the reason for the request. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
Students registered with Student Support and Wellbeing Services / SAP
In the School of Social Science, extensions granted on the basis of registration with Student Support and Wellbeing Services (SSWS) or a Student Access Plan (SAP) will be the lesser of 7 calendar days or the maximum extension allowable for the assessment task.
More information is provided in Part D of the Assessment Procedure.
Discretionary Extensions
You are eligible for a discretionary extension for a single assessment task per semester of study for a duration of 48 hours, with no documentation required. You can apply if the assessment task allows (see the course profile for details) and if it is your first extension request for that assessment.
You cannot receive an extension using both discretionary grounds and SAP grounds for the same assessment and must select one basis when making their initial request.
Maximum Extension Requests
You can request a maximum of 3 extensions for the same assessment task. If a third extension is necessary, you must submit an Assessment Management Plan in addition to your supporting documentation with your request. If after the third approved extension you submit the assessment late, late penalties will apply.
Late Extension Applications
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Additional information
Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team. Course Coordinators are unable to grant extensions directly.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation to the next graduation period.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Assessments will not be accepted if it is more than one week (7 calendar days) late without prior approval.

learning_activities.topic: What is cultural heritage? We will look at what cultural heritage is, the diversity of heritage and archaeology's place in it. We look at how heritage developed, its legislative context and the central role of The Burra Charter in providing a framework for heritage practice. We also briefly cover what you need to know about the course, with a focus on assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
The Burra Charter The Burra Charter is an important document that forms the basis of most heritage work in Australia. Work through the module at your own pace during the week. Learning outcomes: L02

Cultural heritage principles An introduction on how we 'do' cultural heritage management. CHM involves engaging with a set of principles that are repeated across projects, using the Burra Charter we'll explore these steps and what they mean. This week's in-class exercise on the heritage steps will provide the basis for later assessment.

The Queensland Heritage Act 1992 and other planning tools Watch this online content so that you can understand important sections of the Federal legislation, QHA1992, and local planning tools. Work through the module at your own pace during the week. Learning outcomes: L01

Significance Assessment We will explore how to assess cultural heritage significance with a critical lens including guest lecture with Jane Willcock from the Anthropology Museum. We will look at values, criteria and thresholds and learn how to write statements of significance with an in-class exercise.

Indigenous heritage and the ACHA2003 Guest lecturer Michael Aird will discuss the Aboriginal Cultural Heritage Act 2003 and how this legislations provides a framework for Indigenous people to be in charge of their cultural heritage. Although, there are many problems with this legislation, in particular with the processes of deciding who are the most appropriate Indigenous community representatives that need to be consulted.

Anthropology Museum visit (on campus) For the second portion of this week's seminar, we will walk to the Anthropology Museum for a special viewing and group discussion of the new exhibition Shields: Design and Functionality. Information from this visit (or from the Queensland Museum in Week 6) provides the basis for the in-class exercise in Week 7.

Communities and stakeholders This week we will examine connecting heritage to who it matters, by exploring co-design and community led projects. This week's in-class exercise will consider understanding the potential conflicts between community and stakeholders (and technical roles in navigating this space).

Queensland Museum Kurilpa visit This week we will meet at the Queensland Museum Kurilpa for a special viewing and discussion of un/Tethered with curator Imelda Miller. Information from this visit (or UQ Anthropology Museum in Week 4) provides the basis for the in-class exercise in Week 7.

GIS module 1: Introduction to GIS Module 1 of 3 on QGIS. Work through the module at your own pace during the week. The Week 9 exercise will build on the skills you gain in these modules and give you the tools to complete your final assessment. Learning outcomes: L03
Mid Semester Break

Interpretation and heritage outcomes We will look at understanding public heritage spaces, exploring different types of heritage outcomes, interpretation, and some of the practicalities of managing our heritage assets (e.g. funding and resourcing constraints). This weeks in-class exercise will use the notes you made in a museum visit from Weeks 4 or 6 to build on your understanding of the five heritage steps. Learning outcomes: L02, L05

GIS module 2: CHM and GIS data Module 2 of 3 on QGIS. Work through the module at your own pace during the week. The Week 9 exercise will build on the skills you gain in these modules and give you the tools to complete your final assessment. Learning outcomes: L03

Communicating Heritage Reports, presentations and client meetings: how do we use communication tools effectively in CHM? We'll spend time this week engaging with report formats, and in an in-class exercise how AI can or could be used (or not) to support your work. This week's class will help you understand the format of the final assessment. Learning outcomes: L02, L05

Cultural landscapes: What is a cultural landscape and why are they so important? We'll also explore how do we use the tools and building blocks of heritage management (e.g. GIS, site inventories, listings, legislation) to map, engage with and manage cultural landscapes. This week's in-class exercise will use QGIS, building on the skills you have learnt so far in the modules.

GIS module 3: Making maps Module 3 of 3 on QGIS. Work through the module at your own pace during the week. This week's exercise will build on the skills you gain in these modules and give you the tools to complete your final assessment. Learning outcomes: L03

Who owns heritage? Who owns heritage? We will explore the different roles of heritage specialists, what is a heritage narrative, who tells stories and why. In this week's in-class exercise we consider how we can write engaging stories that are perhaps not our own.

Being in landscapes: Moorooka Aboriginal Camp In the context of 'desktop assessment' and the digital age of data accessibility, we'll explore what defines a 'place' and how visiting places and talking to community is critical to understanding cultural heritage. Clear and authentic communication is a critical element to site visits that will also be a central theme to this week's excursion.

Critical heritage studies We reflect on the origins of CHM and the impacts of that and explore some critical approaches to heritage. In this week's in-class exercise we will consider Authorised Heritage Discourse and its use in understanding our authorising heritage environment.
Working in cultural heritage We explore the ethics of heritage and career options. In this week's in-class exercise we will establish our own heritage values to guide future work.
